SUNDAY’S MATCHES.

Next Sunday's matches are all of en-
grossing interest. At Wexford, Dublin
und Wexford clash in football, and the
Metropolitans should give the all-Ireland
Gralists 2 good run. In fact, relying on
the rumours of weakness onthe Dublin
team, the Dubliners feel confident of their
ability to reverse the decision obtained by
Wexiord in last year's Provincial Cham-
pionship. At Birr, Leix and Offaly are
meeting in senior hurling, and while
opinion is favourable to a win for Leix,
the Offaly hurlers are quite competent of
making a bold bid for honours.

At Limerick, Clare are meeting the
homesters in both hurling and football
This is the first outing for Clare since

"y won the All-Ireland Championship,
ind the closest attention will be directed
towards their “form”? with a view to
ascertaining how. they will fare in the
coming Championships. Limerick ought
to win in football, but the hurling is very
open.

THE DEATH OF JACK GRACE.

‘A meeting of the Leinster Council was
held on Sunday at Croke Park, Mr. J. J
Hogan (chairman) presiding. Others
present—Messrs. T. Keating (Athy), D.
J. Burke (Dublin), P. Colgan (Maynooth),
and W. Hanrahan, secretary. Alderma:
Nowlan, President of the Association, was
also present.

‘An application from Kinnegad to have
portion of their parish included in Co.
Meath for the purposes of the Association
‘was referred to the annual Convention on
the 28th iast.

An application Tor permission for a
tournament in Edenderry was referred to
the King’s County Committee to report on.

The Dublin v. Kilkenny hurling match
in the Wolfe Tone Memorial Tourna-
ment, which was postponed from Sunday
as a'mark of respect to the mempry of
the late Me. John Grace, will be re-fixed
at a meeting’on the 28th inst.

The Chairman proposed—" That we,
the Leinster Couneil of the Gaelic Athletic
Association, tender to the family of the
Iate Mr, John Grace our most respectful
sympathy in their great bereavement,
caused by his widely lamented death, and
that we place on record our appreciation
Of the great services that Mr. Grace had
rendered to the Association, both as a
great exponent of hurling and. football
and as a sterling supporter of the Asso-
ciation, That copies of this resolution
be sent to his father and to his brother,
Dr. Pierce Grace, and to Mr. D, J. Gorey,
chairman, Kilkenny County Committee.”*

lution, said: By the death of Jack Grace
we have, lost one of the most brilliant
exponents of our National games. As a
